does: HLS-Seek develops a reinforcement learning framework for generating hardware description code that optimizes both functional correctness and quality of results (QoR) metrics like latency and resource utilization. The core innovation replaces expensive synthesis-in-the-loop RL with a comparative proxy reward model trained to predict which code variants produce better hardware outcomes, achieving 99.53% accuracy in identifying Pareto-dominant designs. To prevent the proxy from gaming its own objective, the method implements uncertainty-aware Monte Carlo dropout switching that selectively invokes real synthesis on low-confidence cases and uses these ground-truth results to continuously improve the proxy model. The approach demonstrates strong results on HLS-eval benchmark, achieving 81.5% syntax correctness and 81.4% functional correctness with a 7B parameter model while providing significant speedup over existing methods.
connects: This work addresses a critical gap in recent LLM-based HLS research, which has focused on functional correctness through standard supervised or instruction-tuned approaches but ignored the hardware-specific optimization objectives that matter in practice. HLS-Seek builds on prior work in synthesis-guided code generation and RL-based program synthesis, but introduces the key insight that HLS evaluation requires only comparative judgments rather than absolute metrics, making it amenable to proxy modeling. The paper connects to broader RL literature on reward learning and addresses the reward hacking problem that emerges when learning from learned reward models, drawing on uncertainty quantification techniques from Bayesian deep learning. It also relates to pragmatic HLS toolchain research that has studied pragma optimization and code structure effects on QoR.
enables: This framework enables practical integration of hardware-aware optimization into neural code generation systems at modest computational cost, opening paths toward autonomous hardware design where large language models generate and optimize implementations end-to-end. The comparative reward approach could generalize to other domains where ground-truth evaluation is expensive but relative comparisons are tractable, such as compiler optimization or circuit design. The uncertainty-aware switching mechanism provides a template for building self-improving systems that balance proxy efficiency with ground-truth fidelity, potentially applicable to other synthesis problems. Future work could explore scaling to larger models, extending to different hardware targets beyond Xilinx Vitis, incorporating additional QoR metrics like power consumption, and developing better proxy architectures that capture hardware-software co-design principles more directly.
